con_certainty: The line figure is drawn in the source (except for the shorter lines between close or small stars) but the shape is not justified. It is a naturalistic human shape with a sash, as the character is named.
con_visual: The Pueblo myth of Long Sash parallels the biblical story of Moses leading the Israelites out of Egypt. Long Sash was a warrior entrusted by the tribe with the task of leading them to a new land where they could live in peace away from destructive neighbouring tribes. The long journey followed The Endless Trail (the Milky Way), and important points in the journey were marked by The Place of Decision (002), The Place of Doubt (003), and The Three Stars of Helpfulness (004).
period: documentation from older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968, in Tewa-speaking villages
place: Tewa-speaking Pueblos in New Mexico: San Ildefonso, Isleta, Jemez, Cochiti, Taos, Nambe
description: none
culture: The Tewa-speaking Eastern Pueblos are a tribe of the Southwest culture area in N America, in the southern Rocky Mountains along Rio Grande and tributaries. A Pueblo is a village made of multistoried dwellings, as well as its people. The Pueblos farmed (mostly corn, squash, and beans), kept turkeys, hunted and gathered, and developed crafts.
astronomy: The Pueblos had a keen interest in the night sky, judging by the many representations of stars in their artwork. In both Pueblo and nearby Navajo creation mythology, a creation figure intends to place the stars in the sky in groups, but another figure ruins this plan and scatters the stars. For the Eastern Pueblos in particular, a creation story tells that the mythical creation mothers created 'star people' and made their eyes out of sparkling white crystal. These people lived in groups, but when the task was given to a mole to carry them in a sack to the upper world, but the mole disobeyed and let the stars fly out and occupy the sky randomly. Only a few of the stars remained in the sack, and these were placed in groups: the Big Dipper, Orion's belt, and the Pleiades. The characters involved vary among stories, as do the star groups (which sometimes include IAU Del, Venus, and other unidentified groups). It is possible that some of the names are Spanish borrowings. No native names are provided in the source [miller1997stars], but some may be documented in the older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968.
data_summary: 12 constellations are identified with variable (but generally low) certainty (of which 10 with line figures), plus names for Venus, the Milky Way, Pleiades, and Canopus. The data comes from pages 174-180 in the source [miller1997stars]. Sky objects 001-005 plus the Milky Way (as The Endless Trail) form a scene corresponding to an exodus myth.
certainty: The author identifies the stars and constellations based on textual documentation from earlier sources. Many of the star identifications are made to a sky region rather than to star names; all the line figures are then tentative and rarely well motivated. When there are multiple names for the same asterism, these may be collected from different villages. Other constellations are named, but not identified: Big Round Circle (representing a dance performed in October, and expected to be a large, irregular group of stars near IAU CrB), Star House (a big constellation in the Western sky after sunset in September), Sandy Corner (a large group of dim stars near IAU Ori), Horned Star (a bright star), Ladder, The Hand (present in other cultures), Wheel, Bull's Eye, Pot-Rest Stars (possibly Orion's belt).

For the Eastern Pueblos in particular, a creation story tells that the mythical creation mothers created 'star people' and made their eyes out of sparkling white crystal. These people lived in groups, but when the task was given to a mole to carry them in a sack to the upper world, but the mole disobeyed and let the stars fly out and occupy the sky randomly. Only a few of the stars remained in the sack, and these were placed in groups: the Big Dipper, Orion's belt, and the Pleiades. The characters involved vary among stories, as do the star groups (which sometimes include IAU Del, Venus, and other unidentified groups). It is possible that some of the names are Spanish borrowings. No native names are provided in the source [miller1997stars], but some may be documented in the older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968.

astronomy: The Pueblos had a keen interest in the night sky, judging by the many representations of stars in their artwork. In both Pueblo and nearby Navajo creation mythology, a creation figure intends to place the stars in the sky in groups, but another figure ruins this plan and scatters the stars. For the Eastern Pueblos in particular, a creation story tells that the mythical creation mothers created 'star people' and made their eyes out of sparkling white crystal. These people lived in groups, but when the task was given to a mole to carry them in a sack to the upper world, but the mole disobeyed and let the stars fly out and occupy the sky randomly. Only a few of the stars remained in the sack, and these were placed in groups: the Big Dipper, Orion's belt, and the Pleiades. The characters involved vary among stories, as do the star groups (which sometimes include IAU Del, Venus, and other unidentified groups). It is possible that some of the names are Spanish borrowings. No native names are provided in the source [miller1997stars], but some may be documented in the older ethnographic sources dated 1916-1968.
data_summary: 12 constellations are identified with variable (but generally low) certainty (of which 10 with line figures), plus names for Venus, the Milky Way, Pleiades, and Canopus. The data comes from pages 174-180 in the source [miller1997stars]. Sky objects 001-005 plus the Milky Way (as The Endless Trail) form a scene corresponding to an exodus myth.
certainty: The author identifies the stars and constellations based on textual documentation from earlier sources. Many of the star identifications are made to a sky region rather than to star names; all the line figures are then tentative and rarely well motivated. When there are multiple names for the same asterism, these may be collected from different villages. Other constellations are named, but not identified: Big Round Circle (representing a dance performed in October, and expected to be a large, irregular group of stars near IAU CrB), Star House (a big constellation in the Western sky after sunset in September), Sandy Corner (a large group of dim stars near IAU Ori), Horned Star (a bright star), Ladder, The Hand (present in other cultures), Wheel, Bull's Eye, Pot-Rest Stars (possibly Orion's belt).

con_certainty: The line figure follows the perimeter of the pictograph provided in the source, so is uncertain. The turtle is composed of four stars for the paws, and one for the head. They are lined as a convex hull.
con_visual: The turtle (CON Thai 001) carries the plough (CON Thai 002) on its back.
description: These are said to be Thai indigenous asterisms, despite early influence from China and India. The imported Indian lunar mansions are also used in Thailand for telling time, but are not included here. There are links to Malay, Java, Bugis, Madura, and Burma asterisms (the turtle and/or plough in Orion). The source interprets a primary source (in Thai) from 2012. Another source for explicitly drawn Thai asterisms was used to clarify some line figures. This was referenced and summarised online by a member of the public [http://archive.astronomerswithoutborders.org/member-reports/285-gam2015/gam-blog/2670-drawing-of-traditional-asterisms-in-thailand.html], is the book 'Tai Baan Doo Dao' (in Thai) by N. Pengkaew (2000), which appears to have some variants for both names and constellations. The Thai sources could not be obtained; only the follow-ups in English, [orchiston2021seasia] and (when the former was not clear) the URL above. Both identify the stars, but draw the constellations with pictographs, not line figures. These are simple enough to transcribe as line figures here following the perimeter of the pictographs.

description: These are said to be Thai indigenous asterisms, despite early influence from China and India. The imported Indian lunar mansions are also used in Thailand for telling time, but are not included here. There are links to Malay, Java, Bugis, Madura, and Burma asterisms (the turtle and/or plough in Orion). The source interprets a primary source (in Thai) from 2012. Another source for explicitly drawn Thai asterisms was used to clarify some line figures. This was referenced and summarised online by a member of the public [http://archive.astronomerswithoutborders.org/member-reports/285-gam2015/gam-blog/2670-drawing-of-traditional-asterisms-in-thailand.html], is the book 'Tai Baan Doo Dao' (in Thai) by N. Pengkaew (2000), which appears to have some variants for both names and constellations. The Thai sources could not be obtained; only the follow-ups in English, [orchiston2021seasia] and (when the former was not clear) the URL above. Both identify the stars, but draw the constellations with pictographs, not line figures. These are simple enough to transcribe as line figures here following the perimeter of the pictographs.

The Toba language belongs to the Guaycurú linguistic group, as do the Mocoví, Abipones, Pilagás and Caduveos. They had a semi-nomadic hunter-gatherer lifestyle until the 19th century. astronomy: Similar to the Mocoví sky culture, although many motifs have switched location in the sky. Native names can vary among the Toba living along the Pilcomayo river and those from eastern Chaco province. Like for the Mocoví, most of the asterisms are within or near the Milky Way. A scene (English: The hunt of the Rhea, Spanish: La Caza del Súri) is formed by 003-005 and the Coalsack Nebula: Two boys and their dogs hunt a rhea; the dogs are ahead and have reached the rhea, and they grab him by the belly. con_lines: none
con_visual: Reflects different myths than 003. 009-010 form a scene. Evil spirits try to kill two little boys; one was killed and became a soul (the fainter star, ζ2_Sco); the other survived (the brighter star, ζ1_Sco). In a later myth, the two are siblings (one boy, one girl) who escaped two old witches (010) who wanted to burn them (the European myth of Hänsel and Gretel).

con_lines: [['* del UMa', '* lam Dra', '* alf UMa', '* bet UMa', '* gam UMa', '* del UMa', '* eps UMa', '* zet01 UMa', '* eta UMa']]
con_visual: The Southern Tutchone say that there is a big-headed man in the sky. The man's big head is the Big Dipper's bowl and a star in front of the bowl. His spine is the handle of the Big Dipper. One day, a hunter shot an arrow (Alcor, not included in this constellation) into his spine. Before he died, the big-headed man said he would reappear into the northern sky at night, and so he does.
description: The Tutchone are a Subarctic tribe in the Yukon plateau in Alaska between the Saint Elias Mountains and the Selwyn Mountains, speaking a language from the Athapaskan family. Subarctic tribes do not have extensive astronomical lore. Only one constellation is documented and drawn, similar to the Big Dipper (with a larger closed bowl), but with a different meaning. The source for this line figure is [miller1997stars] page 83-84. This is attributed to [mcclellan1975myoldpeople], the results of ethnographic research carried out from 1949-51 among three linguistically distinct groups in the southern Yukon Territory (Tlingit, Tagish, and Southern Tutchone).

con_visual: This small constellation appears low in the southern sky but is clearly visible especially from the Zinacanteco hamlets located on the edge of the escarpment overlooking the Grijalva Valley to the south. According to some Zinacantecos the Cross (001) is thought to have been the cross on which Christ was crucified (which rose to the sky after his death), and the two Thieves' Crosses (006, 007) on each side of the Southern Cross are the crosses on which the two thieves were crucified.
place: Zinacantán, in the southern Mexican state of Chiapas
description: none
culture: The Tzotzil are a modern, indigenous Maya people of the central Chiapas highlands in southern Mexico. As of 2000, they numbered under 300,000. The municipalities with the largest Tzotzil population are Chamula, San Cristóbal de las Casas, and Zinacantán (24,300), in the Mexican state of Chiapas.
astronomy: The Zinacantecos don't differentiate among stars, planets, and constellations; all are called k'anal (yellowish one). They are much more informed about the southern (rather than northern) sky, because their view of the northern sky is blocked by the high escarpment and mountains to the north, often covered in clouds. They thus do not see Polaris. Constellations 001-003, 009, and the Pleiades are commonly recognised by the Tzotzil of Zinacantán. 004-008 are identified only by older, more knowledgeable people. The continuity from classic to the modern Maya astronomy is impressive, although the people have had no astronomers any more (as the Maya did). In the intervening 500 years, they also lost their written documents containing the complete zodiacal constellations. The elders still use constellations to accurately tell time at night. The people continue to believe that many celestial bodies are living beings, much as the Maya did. However, the names given to these gods come from 16th-century Spanish Catholicism; crosses were also important symbolism to the Maya though. Their narratives synthesise pre-colonial and Christian themes.
data_summary: The sources provide information on the astronomy of the Tzotzil of Zinacantán only, using other primary sources from the 20th century. The leading source is the paper [vogt1997zinacanteco], which covers the Tzotzil of Zinacantán. The book [milbrath2000star] has (on pages 37-40 and 260+) a summary of contemporary Maya constellations (of the Tzotzil of Zinacantán, and others); this is used to clarify some of the data. 9 constellations are identified (with line figures), plus names for many sky objects. Almost all form a coherent scene, with both natural and Christian themes.
certainty: There is very good certainty for the stars, and clear descriptions of the geometry. Only the stars and line figure for the Scorpion (009) are uncertain. There is an unidentified constellation: the Shonob h'elek' (possibly IAU Del), the high-backed sandals of the demons who killed Christ.

However, the names given to these gods come from 16th-century Spanish Catholicism; crosses were also important symbolism to the Maya though. Their narratives synthesise pre-colonial and Christian themes.
data_summary: The sources provide information on the astronomy of the Tzotzil of Zinacantán only, using other primary sources from the 20th century. The leading source is the paper [vogt1997zinacanteco], which covers the Tzotzil of Zinacantán. The book [milbrath2000star] has (on pages 37-40 and 260+) a summary of contemporary Maya constellations (of the Tzotzil of Zinacantán, and others); this is used to clarify some of the data. 9 constellations are identified (with line figures), plus names for many sky objects. Almost all form a coherent scene, with both natural and Christian themes.
certainty: There is very good certainty for the stars, and clear descriptions of the geometry. Only the stars and line figure for the Scorpion (009) are uncertain. There is an unidentified constellation: the Shonob h'elek' (possibly IAU Del), the high-backed sandals of the demons who killed Christ.

con_certainty: It is uncertain, but put together by [lehmann1923matacos] from bits of information and the meaning of the names of individual parts. On the other hand, later researchers claim that they have found no trace of this asterism.
con_visual: [lehmann1923matacos] The Great Yulo is a mythological bird, the master of the rain, which has an asterism composed of the Pleiades (head, represented by eta Tau alone --- BUT should be the entire Pleiades), the Hyades (body), gam Ori (heels), and Orion's belt (feet). In another narrative (primary sources 1973-1988), this asterism is formed when some corzuela deer flee to the sky to escape their murderous jaguar grandfather. The deer retreat to safety in the sky forming the Patsezlai, the Pleiades; the deer's mother also climbs to the sky as IAU Cru. This myth shows Wichí kinship rules.
place: informants (1921-) originally from Salta Province, Argentina; some interviewed in Ledesma or Orán, Argentina, where indigenous tribes worked during the harvest
description: none
culture: The Wichí (also widely known as the Mataco) are hunter-gatherers from the Mataco-Maká language family (together with the Chorote, Maká, Nivaclé); the population is 40-50,000. They live in Argentina (provinces Salta, Formosa, and Chaco) and Bolivia (Gran Chaco province), neighbouring the Toba in the Pilcomayo River area. Since the beginning of the 20th century, many are Christians, having been much more receptive to the new religion than the Toba.
astronomy: The Wichí say that, formerly, sky and earth were connected by a big tree, so hunters were able to climb the tree and go hunting in the world above --- until an old man burned the tree, and the hunters remained stranded in the sky as the Pleiades. The inhabitants of the sky are non-human powerful beings in a mythical time, with whom a balanced relationship is essential for Wichí survival. This now applies also to spirits introduced by Christianity. Celestial objects are significant in the Wichí world view. The Moon (male) and Venus (female) are said to be married, and the parents of the stars. Similarly to Mocoví traditions, stars (native: kates or katäs) are generally seen as women, whom terrestrial women descend from. The first mothers of the Wichí descended from the stars, so the stars are associated with human fertility, as well as plant (see Pleiades). Some stars which are important for other tribes have no significance for the Wichí (for example, alf-bet Cen).
data_summary: There are 5 constellations (of which 3 with line figures) and many names for other sky objects. Scenes are formed by 001 and the Coalsack Nebula, by 002, the Milky Way, and the Magellanic Clouds, by 003-004 and the Coalsack Nebula. Primary sources in [mariani2017look] from throughout the 20th century. These primary sources include [metraux1939matako,lehmann1923matacos] which provide much of the information.
certainty: There is little and inconsistent astronomical data. Native names (variants and spellings) vary widely between authors and even between informants to the same author. Not identified: [metraux1939matako myth 12] Near the Ostrich (the Constellation of the Ostriches or the Pleiades) there is a leg in the sky. The Pleiades patseLai are the people who formerly inhabited the earth and who now live in the heavens. They warm themselves round a log which is constantly burning in the sky. The fire runs lengthwise along the log and never goes out. If any one should touch that log it would explode. It is of palo santo wood and therefore burns very slowly. The ashes fall on the ground and constitute frost. Also, a corral: [lehmann1923matacos] This is similar to the corrals of the Ava Guaraní, but it is not clear which sky location this is. It is also not clear what other meanings IAU Cru has (there appear to be some).

con_visual: Humanoid constellation composed of 11 groups of named stars that span 76 degrees across the sky. It is said that an ordinary person shot him with an arrow, and the impact broke his back, which is why he has his hands thrown above his head with fingers splayed out. He kicked his legs and feet into the air, which pushed the sky up to where it is now. His head (ups UMa), the place where he was hit with the arrow (or the arrow itself) (g UMa), and the quiver for arrows (tet Boo) are unconnected.

place: on the Zuni river on the Colorado Plateau
description: The Zuni are Western Pueblos in SW North America, who live on the Zuni river on the Colorado Plateau. They illustrate stars on their ceremonial objects (masks, headdresses, altars, murals). After the sun and moon, the most revered celestial body is the Morning Star, formerly the patron of their warriors and hunters. They also recognise and use both Dippers, Orion, the Pleiades, the Milky Way, the North Star and the Evening Star. Using the moon, Orion, and the Pleiades, they mark the time at night, and mark events in the agricultural cycle (such as the planting of corn). The source [miller1997stars] covers the Zuni on pages 180-184, referencing many sources from the period 1900-1981. Most constellations are poorly or incompletely identified in terms of their stars, and few of the line figures are certain. The Zuni had a sky-wide constellation called Chief of the Night. This is described (in unpublished field notes (1929) by the linguist and ethnologist John P. Harrington) as 'the most majestic constellation ever reported from any tribe of Indians. It is a gigantic human figure, bigger than the whole visible sky. Some part of the body of the Chief of the Night is perpetually visible, guarding the earth throughout the year'. This is similar to the whole-sky figures of the Northern Dene cultures in Alaska (Ahtna, Gwich'in, and others). Little of this constellation has been identified in his notes. Later researchers have made a tentative identification of the heart (CON Zuni 007) and arms (CON Zuni 008-009). In addition to these (partly) identified constellations, there is an unidentified one: Stick Game Ones (presumably located near IAU Lib), which is associated with a game played by the Zuni priesthood to conjure rain.
